title: Juniper and cedarwood soy jar candle
subtitle: A 200 ml container candle with a fresh piney top note and a warm dry wood base
excerpt: Juniper berry essential oil brings a fresh, piney, slightly resinous top note; cedarwood anchors it with a warm, dry woody base. The two oils together evoke a cold-forest character that carries well in a soy container candle. A CD-12 wick in a 200 ml glass jar gives a 25 to 30-hour burn.
sourceNotes: Soy wax container candle technique: Candlescience wick sizing guide (2023); CD-12 for 6 to 7 cm jar diameter. Fragrance load at 8% by wax weight: maximum for GW 464 soy wax. Juniper berry EO (Juniperus communis, steam-distilled berry): fresh, piney, slightly citrus-green. Cedarwood EO (Cedrus atlantica or Juniperus virginiana): warm, dry, woody base note. Both reported toxic to cats.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 x 200 ml jar candle (130 g soy wax fill)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale weights by the number of jars. One pre-tabbed wick per jar.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure for 3 to 5 days before burning. Woody resinous blends can improve noticeably with a longer cure.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Wax stable for 5 years or more. Cedarwood and juniper both have reasonable longevity in a candle; the blend should remain recognisable for 2 years. Keep the lid on between burns.

glossaryTerms.definition: The first burn of a candle sets the wax memory: the melt pool diameter on the first burn becomes the maximum it will reach on subsequent burns. Burning to the full jar diameter on the first burn prevents tunnelling.
The temperature at which liquid wax is poured into the container. Soy container wax is poured at 55 to 65°C; too hot and the wax pulls away from the sides; too cool and it sets unevenly.

content.text: Juniper berry essential oil is distilled from the berries rather than the wood, which gives it a different character to the conifer-needle oils: it is fresh, piney, and slightly berry-citrus rather than resinous. Cedarwood provides the warm, dry, woody base that gives the blend depth and hold. Together they sit well in soy wax and give a cold-forest scent that is particularly at home in winter. Both oils are reported toxic to cats; keep the candle away from rooms where cats spend time.

items.prepNote: Container-blend soy wax; GW 464 or equivalent
4.2% of wax weight; fresh, piney top note
3.8% of wax weight; warm, dry woody base note; toxic to cats
CD-12 for a 6 to 7 cm diameter jar

content.text: Place the soy wax in the candle pouring pitcher and set in a pan of barely simmering water. Heat, stirring occasionally, until fully melted and at 80°C. Remove from the heat and allow to cool.
Secure the sustainer tab of the pre-tabbed wick to the centre of the jar base. Thread the wick through the centring bar and rest the bar across the jar rim so the wick hangs plumb in the centre.
Add the essential oils and pour
When the wax has cooled to 60°C, add the juniper berry and cedarwood essential oils and stir for 2 minutes. Pour at
: between 55 and 65°C. Pour in a slow, steady stream down the side of the jar, leaving a 5 to 10 mm gap at the top. Leave undisturbed until fully set.
Cure for at least 1 day before burning; 3 to 5 days gives the best fragrance throw. Woody resinous blends often improve noticeably with a longer cure. Trim the wick to 5 mm before each burn.
. Burn until the melt pool reaches the full jar diameter (3 to 4 hours). Do not burn in a draught.
Keep the lid on between burns. Wax stable for 5 years or more; fragrance should remain recognisable for about 2 years. Toxic to cats; keep away from rooms where cats are present.
